path: 04 Resources/ETFA25 AAS Paper/ETFA25-000419 Capability Determination in Manufacturing Using Historical Product Data.pdf
text: Capability Determination in Manufacturing Using Historical Product Data Marco Simon∗‡, Patrick Kremser†, Tatjana Legler†‡, Martin Ruskowski∗†‡ ∗Technologie-Initiative SmartFactory KL e. V. (SFKL), Kaiserslautern, Germany †University of Kaiserslautern-Landau (RPTU), Kaiserslautern, Germany ‡German Research Center for Artificial Intelligence (DFKI), Kaiserslautern, Germany Abstract—The increasing dynamics and complexity of today’s manufacturing environments require greater flexibility and re- silience to meet the demands of producing multi-variant products, including batch size one, with minimal additional effort. The Capability, Service and Skill Model provides an answer to this problem by decoupling processes and the resources used. However, especially small and medium-sized enterprises face the challenge of systematically modeling their machines and systems in terms of capabilities and skills, as the required knowledge is often not available in a structured form and is often tied to individual key personnel. To ensure a consistent and practical description of capabilities, a method based on historical data is proposed to help companies integrate their manufacturing resources into a networked system of manufacturing service providers. On this basis, historical manufacturing features are systematically examined to evaluate the potential to use them to determine capabilities. Index Terms—Industry 4.0, Capability Modeling, Resource function, Manufacturing Features, Asset Administration Shell, Manufacturing Ontology I. INTRODUCTION To increase flexibility and resilience, it is advantageous for machines and systems to be able to produce a wide range of product variants without significant preparation for small batch sizes. The capability, service and skill (CSS) Model [1] was developed to achieve the necessary flexibility in companies. The model decouples the rigid link between processes and resources by introducing the production function at different levels of a capability, a service and a skill. A capability is defined as the implementation-independent specification of a function [2]. It contains a technical description of the pro- duction function without naming the specific implementation of the machine. To use the CSS model and the capability description effectively, the capabilities required for production must be assigned to the products and the capabilities to be offered must be assigned to the resources. This makes it possible to match the individual process steps required to produce a product or sub-product with the corresponding capabilities of a machine. Consequently, a sequence of pro- duction steps is created that leads to the manufacture of a product. Determining capabilities is particularly challenging for small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) because in practice the relevant knowledge about machines and systems is often implicit, for example in the form of past product data or in the experience of individual key personnel who may no longer be available to the company. In addition, the necessary knowledge of the technologies to be used and the modeling skills are not necessarily available. To enable these companies to offer consistently formulated capabilities, they should be given an onboarding process. This will allow them to be integrated into a potential network of manufacturing service providers, allowing them to leverage their unique com- petencies. In this paper, a methodical approach is developed to derive the capabilities systematically from historical data and thus provide an onboarding concept for SMEs. By drawing on existing know-how and actual manufacturing experience from the past, reliable statements can be made about the actual machine capabilities, even for older or partially manually op- erated systems. Section II presents current capability modeling approaches and existing methods for determining capabilities. Section III analyses and evaluates these methods regarding their applicability in determining capabilities from historical data. This evaluation is based on the usability of the methods, considering the respective data requirements. In Section IV, the selected method is implemented using a practical example. Specific data sources are used for this purpose, namely the Standard for the Exchange of Product Model Data (STEP) [3] and Siemens NX Features [4], which are used to realize the methodology. The following sections provide a discussion of the results and an outlook. II. RELATED WORK The first section provides an overview of the approaches that have been investigated for capability modeling. When modeling, it is also important to follow a methodology to ensure consistency and reusability. The need to ensure that the functions of a machine are consistent and can be accessed formally is well documented in the literature, as presented in the second section. A. Capability Modeling Approaches In [5], [6], an approach is presented to evaluate large language models (LLMs) for their suitability to generate capability ontologies. Two LLMs are compared using three different prompting techniques (zero-shot, one-shot, and few- shot prompting) to generate multiple capability ontologies. The prompt provides the instruction, the context, optionally none, one, or more examples, and a task description in which the capability is described with its properties and constraints using natural language. The input for a capability description in this case is therefore a natural language description. The modeling of capabilities is also addressed in the final report of the Administration Shell for the Line Set (VWS4LS) project [7]. For example, the required capability is derived from the product specification. A sample product is presented that is used to derive the required capabilities and their parameters from a product description. The resource and the tools used define the offered capabilities. However, automation of such a process is not discussed in the final report. All required and offered modeled capabilities and their properties are listed in the appendix [7]. The technology used is the current version of the Capability Description submodel 1 of Industrial Digital Twin Association (IDTA) at the time of the project. Another approach [8] uses existing standards such as DIN 8580 [9], which describes a taxonomy for manufacturing processes, and VDI 2860 [10], which standardizes terms and symbols for handling operations, to formulate abstract capa- bilities. The capabilities are described as processes according to VDI 3682 [11]. An alignment ontology is developed, in which the class Cap:Capability serves as an alias for VDI 3682:Process. Both taxonomies are modeled as subclasses of the Cap:Capability class. This approach is therefore intended to create the abstract capabilities from the existing taxonomies without creating concrete instances of machines. In [12], a modeling tool for capability-based engineering is presented. The capabilities are modeled using the AAS technology to enable flexible production lines. The concept is tested with a production cell containing a robot, a drilling machine, and a painting system. The modeling tool supports the capability modeler in creating the capabilities according to the AAS metamodel. However, the modeler must be familiar with the technical properties and their limitations. B. Methodology for Determining Capabilities Consistent modeling of capabilities is not a trivial task, as pointed out in [13]. The CSS Model Extensions and Engineering Whitepaper [14] identifies different stakeholders involved in this task and highlights the challenge that in- adequate documentation or technical specifications can sig- nificantly slow down the modeling process. Similarly, [15], which provides a comprehensive overview of capability- and skill-based manufacturing, identifies inconsistently modeled capabilities and skills as a key barrier to their application. Against this background, there is a clear need to describe capabilities using formal methods based on heterogeneous in- formation sources. Therefore, current methods for determining capabilities are considered in this section. The works in [14], [16] present methodological approaches for the systematic description, assignment, and derivation of capabilities. In addi- tion, a superordinate methodology is developed that integrates the three technology-independent methods. 1https://industrialdigitaltwin.org/content-hub/teilmodelle — last accessed: May 23, 2025. The Describing Capability method ensures a formal, se- mantic description of capabilities. It also specifies the context in which the respective capability can be applied. Reference [16] emphasizes that this context can be refined both through information from production processes and through interviews with relevant stakeholders. To avoid redundancies and du- plications in the modeling, a comparison is performed with existing databases or publicly accessible sources. If no matches with existing descriptions are identified, possible sources and standards that can be used to describe the capability are determined. Based on this, the capability concept is formal- ized, dependencies on other capabilities as well as individual properties and constraints are defined. Finally, the elements are semantically assigned to enable later identification and reuse of the capability. The Assigning Capability method addresses the assignment of capabilities to a specific production resource. The aim is to enable manufacturers and operators to derive instances of a capability model for their machines. Once a resource is identi- fied, the skills implemented on the machine are extracted. As described in [16], this approach assumes that a skill description already exists for the respective machine. Recognizing and describing the skills is of central importance, as without a corresponding implementation, the information models of the skill description cannot be used as input for the further process. However, if a skill description is available, the effect of the respective skill can be derived from it. An approach for the formal formulation of such effects is presented in [17]. If a suitable formal skill description exists for the identified effect, an adapted version of this description is assigned to the resource. If no suitable description is available, a placeholder is created that serves as the basis for generating a new formal capability description. The Deriving Capability method aims to systematically de- rive the capabilities required to manufacture a product or carry out a process from existing product or process descriptions. In the first step, the relevant effects are identified based on the existing product specification or process description. Similar to the Assigning Capability method, an attempt is made to assign a suitable formal capability description. If such a description exists, an adapted version of the product specification or process description is assigned. If no suitable formal capability description exists, a capability placeholder is created, which serves as the basis for the subsequent development of a new formal description. III. METHODOLOGY The methods presented should make it possible to sys- tematically determine the capabilities of company resources based on historical data. The historical data used can include, for example, previous product and process data, recorded machine parameters, tool data or technical data sheets. In the case of product data, the Deriving Capability method according to [16] can be used. However, this method is not suitable for other types of data sources, such as tools used, recorded machine parameters or the machine data sheets valid at the time of use. To assign capabilities to a resource, for example in the context of a machine manufacturer or operator, [16] refers to the Assigning Capability method. However, this assumes that the resource under consideration already has a skill implementation from which the existing capabilities can be derived. If such a skill implementation is not available or if alternative sources of information are to be used, the existing method reaches its limits. There are therefore a number of reasons for extending the model. For example, many older or manually operated systems do not necessary have skill implementations. This means that the knowledge required to control the resource correctly often resides in the heads of individuals who operate such systems manually. Another reason may be a lack of automation or the absence of important interfaces. In addition, the white paper [2] points out that a skill is not necessarily a prerequisite for a capability. In certain use cases, a capability can also be realized without an associated skill. Such cases are not taken into account by the current status of the Assigning Capability method. Figure 1 illustrates the extension of the existing Assigning Capability method within the steps highlighted in green. The aim of the extended methodology is to demonstrate how capabilities can be assigned to a resource, particularly in cases where no explicit skill implementations exist for the machines. In such cases, alternative data-based information sources directly related to the resource can be utilized to systematically derive the relevant capabilities. Assigning Capabilities - Extended Identify resource Identify resource skill(s) Skill description [MTP, PackML, CC, ...] Input Identify effect for resource skill Assign capability to resource [Capability identified][No capability identified] (Production) Resource [Machine, Human, ...] Select capability Create temporary capability placeholder Selected formal capability [positioning, ...] Input OutputFormal capability description [DIN 8580, TGL 25000, Customized] Derive required information for capability selection Effect description [Change of: product geometry, process temperatur] Standards for process (step) description [DIN 8580, TGL 25000 and other documents] Input Input Output Output Temporary capability list [positioning, drilling, ...] Adapt formal capability for resource Identify other resource specific data [No ressource skill to identify] Extraction and structuring of the resource specific data Input Output [ressource skill to identify] (Historical) Data data sheet, historic machine parameters, tooling data, .. Input Input Fig. 1: Workflow for the extended method of Assigning Capabilities based on [16] Once the resource has been identified, the Assigning Ca- pability – Extended method determines whether implemented skills exist. If this is the case, the method can proceed as described in [16]. However, if no implemented skills are available, alternative historical, resource-related data can be utilized. This typically includes operational data recorded during machine usage, such as historical machine data or tool data, which can be processed and structured in subsequent steps. Additionally, machine data sheets represent a relevant data source. These data sheets may be updated throughout the machine’s service life, for example, after system modi- fications or upgrades, and can thus be regarded as a form of historical documentation. Beyond the mentioned sources, further relevant data sources may exist. Once the relevant data has been identified and collected, it is systematically processed to extract information required for capability selection in the subsequent step. The revised method is then used as a part to present the higher level method Determining Capabilities based on historical data (see Figure 2). The Determining Capabilities Method presented in [16] is sufficiently generic so that it can also be applied to use cases in which capabilities are derived based on historical data. The method shown in Figure 2 was developed to illustrate this special use case and make the methodical approach comprehensible. It illustrates the specific process of using historical data sources and the decision- making processes required. The connections and process steps defined in the context of this method are highlighted in green for better illustration. Collect (historical) data regarding a resource Deriving Capabilities Assigning Capabilities - extended Assigning a formal capability or a temporary capability placeholder to the resource Describing Capabilities Resolve all existing capability placeholder Identify the resource where the parts were producedInput (Historical) Data Product specification, Process description, data sheet, historic machine parameters, tooling data [product or process specific data] [ressource specific data] [Capability to describe] [Capability not to describe] [more data to process] [no more data to process] Input (Production) Resource [Machine, Human, ...] Input Temporary capability list [positioning, drilling, ...] Formal capability description [DIN 8580, TGL 25000, Customized] Input/Output Output Input/Output Input InputInput Determining Capabilities based on historical data Fig. 2: Workflow for the method Determining Capabilities based on historical data This method can be used by companies, for example, to go through a structured onboarding process with the aim of consistently recording and describing their capabilities. On this basis, they can offer their capabilities in a potential network of production service providers in a standardized manner. The aim of the method is therefore to identify the capabilities provided by machines in operation and to transfer them into a formalized form. As a starting point, it is assumed that historical data sources are known and available, and that they can be uniquely associated with individual resources, such as the machines of a manufacturing service provider. The first step is to collect the relevant historical data. The next step is to classify the data, as the further methodical process depends on the type of information available. If a resource-specific dataset is available, the Assigning Capabilities - Extended method is used. If, on the other hand, the data are product or process specific, the first step is to identify the associated resource on which the relevant products were manufactured. The Deriving Capability method is then used. If the application of the method results in the derivation of a formal capability, it is then assigned to the corresponding resource. Otherwise, the assignment step is skipped. As shown in [16], both of these methods lead to a result in the form of a formal capability or a temporary placeholder for a capability. If a capability has already been formally described and no further relevant data is available, the method is completed. If additional data is available, the process is restarted from the initial decision. Conversely, when generating a capability placeholder, a more detailed description is required. IV. IMPLEMENTATION In the following, an approach is presented that is able to aggregate capabilities from a historical data source and thus realize the method Determining Capabilities based on historical data. The aggregated capabilities refer to machines or resources and describe their potential capabilities. It is assumed that the products manufactured on a machine allow conclusions to be drawn about its capabilities. Consequently, the products historically produced by a company reflect its capabilities in an aggregated form. As previously outlined, various data sources can be utilized to formally describe capabilities. The following outlines the individual steps of the proposed method applied to a specific data source. A. Collecting Historical Data Regarding a Resource In the following, it is assumed that historical data is only available as a product specification in the form of a CAD (computer-aided design) model. The decision to apply the method based on CAD data does not preclude the use of other data sources for validation. In the present approach, only CAD data is considered, as it is commonly used to transfer orders to contract manufacturers. If additional data sources, such as material data, are employed, the method remains applicable but may require adaptations in certain procedural steps. The exemplary implementation focuses on the CAD models which are available in the so-called Standard for the Exchange of Product Model Data (STEP) [3] or in proprietary Siemens NX format [4]. STEP is a standard for exchanging product data between different systems. EXPRESS, formalized in ISO 10303-11 [18], is used as the description language for the exchange of product data in STEP. Unlike the open standards in STEP, features in the NX format are described using a proprietary Siemens data model. An example product is selected as a representative historical CAD model that has been produced by a company. The example product is a rectangular polyoxymethylene (POM) solid with eight holes and two rectangular pockets. The raw part has dimensions of 31.8 mm along the X-direction, 95.8 mm along the Y-direction, and 35.2 mm along the Z-direction. The positioning of the features is shown in Figure 3. Fig. 3: Image of the sample workpiece with eight drilled holes and two rectangular pockets B. Identify the Resource Where the Parts Were Produced After the historical data has been collected using the product shown in Figure 3 as an example, a classification is made whether the data is product, process or resource-specific. As the CAD model is product-specific data, the next step in the method is to identify the resource where the parts were produced. This assignment is necessary to be able to assign the capability of the resource based on the analyzed product data in a later step. The resource used in the present context is a robot cell that is operated at the Kaiserslautern campus of the Rhineland-Palatinate Technical University of Kaiserslautern- Landau (RPTU). After successful identification, the method Deriving Capabilities is applied. C. Deriving Capabilities To be able to derive relevant information for a capability description from this data and to map it in a structured manner, it is necessary to increase the formality and expressiveness of the available data, see Figure 4. This is here achieved within the method Deriving Capabilities from [16]. As the features in the Siemens NX data model allow additional functions such as a feature history, this data model is more expressive than EXPRESS. However, in terms of formality, EXPRESS was ranked higher by the authors becauseFormality Expressivity Data Models EXPRESS NX Data Model Information Models UML Knowledge Models Web Ontology Language Deriving Capability Fig. 4: Representation of data, information and knowledge models along the axes of formality and expressivity based on [19]. Siemens NX data model does not use a formal description language like EXPRESS. Figure 4 illustrates this. The Figure does not make a quantitative statement, as the formality and expressivity axes are not supported by numerical values. Instead, it is intended to make a qualitative statement about the visual classification of the three groupings ”data models”, ”information model” and ”knowledge model”. The Unified Modelling Language (UML) [20] is included in the informa- tion model category. UML is considered a more expressive and formal modeling language, as it is used across various domains. The type of information model is typically used to represent complete processes or, within the software domain, to support the design and implementation of software-based systems [20]. The Web Ontology Language (OWL) [21], by contrast, falls within the category of knowledge models. It is regarded as particularly expressive due to its foundation on the open-world assumption (OWA) and its support for automatic reasoning. OWA assumes that a lack of knowledge is not necessarily to be interpreted as false. Instead, it assumes that a statement could be true even if it is not explicitly contained in the available knowledge. UML, on the other hand, is geared towards data modelling, where the knowledge represented is implicitly assumed to be complete. Furthermore, its model- theoretic semantics contribute to a high degree of formal precision [22]. The Deriving Capability method starts with the identifica- tion of the product specification or a given process description. In this use case, the CAD model of the example product is the key input. As this is a product specification, the path Deriving capabilities from product specification is followed. The use case under consideration is a single product without a modular structure. Accordingly, all product components are already clearly identified. The next step in the method is to identify the effect of the product. A typical example, as described in [14] is the removal of material to create a defined geometry, such as a hole. This information is incorporated into the CAD model through the use of specific manufacturing features. Rec- ognizing the manufacturing features therefore leads directly to the identification of the resulting effects. STEP defines several Application Protocols (APs) to facilitate the exchange of data between different application domains [3]. It is modular and structured into individual APs, with AP 242 serving as the primary standard for representing manufacturing features, such as rectangular pockets and holes, in the STEP format. Despite its functionalities, previous studies [23] and [24] identified a lack of suitable feature export and extraction tools based on AP 242 on the market. To fill this gap, a Python-based plug- in for Siemens NX 1872 was developed, focusing on feature extraction shown in Figure 5. Fig. 5: Image of the Python plugin with the ’Find Fea- tures’ button highlighted in red and the parameter of the Pocket Rectangular Straight 3 feature displayed. Using the ’Find Features’ function in the NX manufacturing view, the plug-in enables parametric recognition of geometric features such as pockets and holes. These features, which are stored in the native Siemens NX data format, include information about the feature types and associated design data. To ensure that manufacturing tolerances are taken into account, the design data is supplemented with NX-embedded Product Manufacturing Information. The plug-in organizes all extracted object information within a Python dictionary structure, facilitating efficient data handling and integration. After completing the feature extraction, all effects on the products, i.e., the changes in product geometry, have been identified and are represented within the extracted geometric features in NX format. In the next step of the Deriving Capability method, pro- cesses and their respective effects are identified based on a production plan. However, this introduces certain limitations. If only the geometric model is considered, process-relevant information cannot be fully captured. Additional data sources, such as production schedules or machine parameters, would be required to include these aspects. Consequently, these aspects are not fully addressed in the current implementation, as they require process data that is not available in the data sources considered. In the next methodological step, the required information for capability selection is derived. To support this process, additional normative specifications, such as DIN 8580 [9], are utilized as supplementary sources of domain knowledge. Within this context, the proprietary NX data format serves as the primary data source. The NX format enables the extrac- tion and structuring of relevant feature information, which is subsequently transformed into a standardized output format suitable for capability selection. In the subsequent capability selection step, a formal capabil- ity is selected from an existing repertoire of formal capability descriptions. It must be ensured that the selected formal capability corresponds to the effects derived from the product specification. DIN 8580, TGL 25000 and company-specific or individual descriptions are examples of possible sources for formal capability descriptions [16]. In this context, the features defined in Siemens NX can be interpreted as individual formal capability descriptors. For example, an NX feature describes a hole that is characterized by numerous parameters and dependencies. In particular, the NX Step1Pocket feature has a set of properties that specify, for example, the shape, position, roughness, and tolerance of the manufactured hole in the product. This proves that the relevant capability parameters are already available for certain features. It furthermore illustrates that the content is consistent with the effects derived from the product specification, as required in [14]. Under this condition, the corresponding capability can be considered identified. The next step is to adapt this capa- bility to the specific product specification. This is achieved by mapping the parameters of the product specification to the structure of the formal Capability Description submodel provided by the IDTA. At the time of writing, the submodel had not yet been officially published. Therefore, reference is made to the development status available at the time of writing. The submodel was designed in UML and can be visualized as a JavaScript Object Notation (JSON) file in the AASX Package Explorer [25]. In this step, the information extracted from the NX data model is integrated into the Asset Administration Shell of the resource, thereby increasing both the level of formalization and the expressiveness of the available information, as shown in Figure 4. The values obtained from the feature extractor are, for example, STEP1Pocket_5 or STEP1Pocket_6. Each of these STEP1Pocket instances contains the same set of up to forty parameters, each assigned a specific value. Depending on the respective STEP1Pocket instance, the parameter values vary, as dimensions such as depth or hole positioning may differ. Using a Python script in combination with the BaSyx Python SDK 2, these parameters and their corresponding val- ues are processed as input and created within a PropertyCon- tainer inside the Capability Description submodel. The varying parameter values are added as list elements to the Property- Container. In this way, the submodel is automatically gener- ated. Figure 6 shows how the information from the Siemens NX Data Model was transferred to the CapabilityDescription submodel. As an example, the parameters X POSITION and Y POSITION have been expanded in Figure 6 to show how the values are modeled as list values. It is important to note that the X POSITION and Y POSITION parameters 2https://github.com/eclipse-basyx/basyx-python-sdk — last accessed: June 01, 2025. are relative to the workpiece coordinate system. However, this information is sufficient for the purpose of checking the feasibility of execution, given that the transformation of machine coordinates to the workpiece coordinate system has been previously established for the execution of this feature. Fig. 6: Visualization of the Offered Capability in the current status of the CapabilityDescription submodel of the Industrial Digital Twin Association As described in Figure 4, the submodel is then transformed into an ontology to increase expressiveness and formality even more. For this purpose, an ontology based on the Capability Description submodel was developed, as shown in Figure 7. The classes and relations previously specified in UML diagrams were converted into classes, object and data type properties using Prot´eg´e, an open-source ontology develop- ment and editing software 2. Fig. 7: Illustration of the Capability Ontology via Proteg´e The information from the Capability Description Submodel was modeled in Prot´eg´e as individuals of corresponding classes. For example, the individual PropertyContainer ele- ments of the “Step1Pocket” capability were created as indi- viduals of the PropertyContainer class. Figure 8 illustrates this 2https://protege.stanford.edu/ - last accessed: April 09, 2025. using the individual PropertyContainer02 as an example. The ontology was visualized using the Prot´eg´e plugin OntoGraf 3. Fig. 8: Exemplary visualization of the PropertyContainer02 via On- toGraf PropertyContainer02 has an object property assertion indi- cating that it contains a submodel element of the type Submod- elElementList with the name Y POSITION. Y POSITION is an individual of the class SubmodelElementList, which in turn is a subclass of the abstract class CapabilityProperty. Figure 9 shows an example of the two individuals X POSITION and Y POSITION. These each have a data property assertion in which the list elements and their associated values are listed. This ensures that no information is lost. If the capability is present within the ontology, the formal capability is fully adapted. This completes the method Deriving Capabilities and moves on to the next step. Fig. 9: Exemplary visualization of the two individuals X POSITION and Y POSITION via OntoGraf D. Assigning a Formal Capability or a Temporary Capability Placeholder to the Resource Once the resource has been uniquely identified, the Ca- pabilityDescription submodel can be assigned to the Asset Administration Shell of the specific resource, which in this use case corresponds to the robot cell operated at the Kaiser- slautern campus. The method Determining Capabilities based on Historical Data terminates at this point, as no further describable capabilities are available, and the dataset has been fully processed. V. DISCUSSION The presented implementation demonstrates a complete ap- plication of the Determining Capabilities based on Historical Data method. As historical data, an existing CAD model was selected as an example, as such data is typically available when 3https://protegewiki.stanford.edu/wiki/OntoGraf - last accessed: April 11, 2025. working with contract manufacturers. The features extracted from the Siemens NX model were transferred into the Capabil- ity Description submodel and subsequently into an ontology. This use case therefore focuses on components modeled using NX, other systems are not supported at this stage. Additional data sources, such as material data, were deliberately excluded in the present implementation to limit complexity and en- able an initial realization. As a result, the limitations of the implementation presented here differ from the fundamental boundaries of the underlying method. In principle, the method can be generalized to other data sources. However, adapting the specific implementation steps is required depending on the selected data source. Furthermore, the aspect of the Effect, as described for example in [17], has not yet been considered in this implementation. This is because the selected data source, i.e. the CAD models and NX features, already contains an implicit description of the effect or does not allow further derivation of the effect beyond geometric properties. If the method is applied to other data sources in the future, the use of the Effect must be re-evaluated. VI. OUTLOOK In this paper, an approach for determining capabilities based on historical data was developed. In the initial phase of the study, existing methods described in the literature were exam- ined. As a result, the Assigning Capabilities method from [16] was extended, and a general method for Determining Capabil- ities based on Historical Data was formulated. As part of this methodological development, it was shown that various histor- ical data sources can potentially serve as input for capability descriptions. The subsequent section focused on the use of Siemens NX features as an exemplary data source for deriving capabilities. It demonstrated that transferring information from the data model through an information model into a knowledge model can enhance both the expressiveness and the degree of formalization of the representation. The implementation demonstrates the derivation of capabilities based on identified manufacturing features, utilizing the Deriving Capabilities method within the superordinate methodological framework. However, the implementation has certain limitations. The extraction of parameter values from features is limited to discrete forms. For instance, if a specific drill diameter is not documented in the available data, it cannot be included in the capability description, even though manufacturing it may still be technically feasible with minor adjustments. In such cases, interpolation techniques or machine learning methods could provide a viable solution. Additionally, process-relevant effects, such as temperature-dependent conditions, cannot be fully addressed when only geometric models are considered. Incorporating further data sources would be necessary to access and integrate such information. For future work, an integrative approach combining multiple data sources could enable the development of more comprehensive and realistic capability models. Furthermore, the resulting ontology could be integrated into a knowledge graph to serve as a basis for automated matching processes. This would allow newly designed products to be automatically evaluated against the existing machine capabilities, supporting decision-making in production planning and resource allocation. ACKNOWLEDGMENT This research has been supported by the European Union’s HORIZON Research and Innovation Action Program under the grant agreement No 101138782, the project RAASCEMAN. REFERENCES [1] A.
